docs.title: GoVEX
docs.text:
Vulnerability management library and CLI toolkit for Go

GoVEX is a comprehensive solution for creating, consolidating, and sharing vulnerability reports across multiple formats including XLSX, CSV, Markdown, JSON, and git repositories.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Generic Vulnerability Structs - Unified format for vulnerability data from various sources
  • Multi-Format Reports - Generate XLSX, CSV, and Markdown reports
  • Vulnerability Website - Create Markdown websites for tracking vulnerabilities across projects
  • CI/CD Integration - Commands with proper exit codes for pipeline integration
  • SLA Management - Track remediation SLAs with severity-based policies
  • Security Letters - Generate SLA exception and hardening notification letters

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
import "github.com/grokify/govex"

// Create a vulnerability set
vulns := govex.Vulnerabilities{
    {
        ID:       "CVE-2024-1234",
        Title:    "SQL Injection in login form",
        Severity: "High",
        Status:   "Open",
    },
}

// Generate a table for reporting
table := vulns.Table(govex.TableColumnDefinitionSetSASTSCAReport())

// Export to XLSX
table.WriteXLSX("vulnerabilities.xlsx")

docs.title: govex homepage
docs.text: Generate a Markdown homepage for vulnerability report websites.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: govex homepage [flags]

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The homepage command creates a Markdown index page for vulnerability tracking websites. This is useful for creating documentation sites that aggregate vulnerability reports across multiple projects or repositories.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Generate landing pages for vulnerability documentation sites
  • Create index files for MkDocs or similar static site generators
  • Build vulnerability dashboards in git-based workflows

docs.title: govex letter
docs.text: Generate security notification letters for vulnerability findings.

docs.title: Usage

docs.text: Command Description generate Generate a letter from JSON or command-line parameters schema Output JSON Schema for the letter format example Output example JSON template

docs.title: govex merge
docs.text: Merge multiple JSON vulnerability files into a single consolidated file.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: govex merge [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --input, -i Input JSON file (can be specified multiple times) --output, -o Output JSON file path

docs.title: Merge Two Files
docs.text: govex merge --input scan1.json --input scan2.json --output combined.json

docs.title: Merge Multiple Scanner Outputs
docs.text:
govex merge \
  --input semgrep-results.json \
  --input spotbugs-results.json \
  --input grype-results.json \
  --output all-vulnerabilities.json

docs.title: Input Format
docs.text:
Input files should contain vulnerability data in GoVEX JSON format:

{
  "vulnerabilities": [
    {
      "id": "CVE-2024-1234",
      "title": "SQL Injection",
      "severity": "High",
      "status": "Open"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Output
docs.text: The merged output combines all vulnerabilities from input files, preserving all fields.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks through common GoVEX workflows.

docs.title: Creating Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
package main

import (
    "github.com/grokify/govex"
)

func main() {
    // Create vulnerability records
    vulns := govex.Vulnerabilities{
        {
            ID:          "CVE-2024-1234",
            Title:       "SQL Injection vulnerability",
            Severity:    "High",
            Status:      "Open",
            Module:      "backend-api",
            Description: "User input not sanitized in login endpoint",
        },
        {
            ID:          "CVE-2024-5678",
            Title:       "XSS in comment field",
            Severity:    "Medium",
            Status:      "In Progress",
            Module:      "frontend",
        },
    }

    // Generate a report table
    table := vulns.Table(govex.TableColumnDefinitionSetSASTSCAReport())

    // Export to different formats
    table.WriteXLSX("report.xlsx")
    table.WriteCSV("report.csv")
}

docs.title: Filtering Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
// Filter by severity
highSeverity := vulns.FilterSeverity("High")

// Filter by module
apiVulns := vulns.FilterModule("backend-api")

// Filter by status
openVulns := vulns.FilterStatus("Open")

docs.title: SLA Tracking
docs.text:
import "github.com/grokify/govex/severity"

// Define SLA policy (days to remediate by severity)
policy := severity.SLAMap{
    "Critical": 7,
    "High":     30,
    "Medium":   90,
    "Low":      180,
}

// Check if a vulnerability is overdue
isOverdue := policy.IsOverdue("High", detectedDate)

  --sla-new-date 2026-07-31 \
  --delay-reason "Dependent on upstream change" \
  --risk "Low likelihood due to internal access" \
  --mitigation "WAF rules in place" \
  --remediation "Implement native rate limiting" \

  --sender-title "Security Engineer" \

docs.title: Integrations Overview
docs.text: GoVEX integrates with popular security scanning tools and vulnerability feeds.

docs.title: Analyzers
docs.text:
Convert output from security analysis tools to GoVEX format.

Tool Package Description Semgrep analyzers/semgrep SAST with CWE/OWASP mapping SpotBugs analyzers/spotbugs Java security analysis

docs.title: Feeds
docs.text:
Ingest vulnerability data from external sources.

Source Package Description CISA KEV feeds/cisakev Known Exploited Vulnerabilities

docs.title: External Integrations
docs.text:
Additional integrations available as separate packages:

Tool Package Description Grype github.com/grokify/gogrype Container vulnerability scanner

docs.title: Integration Pattern
docs.text:
All analyzers follow a similar pattern:

// 1. Parse tool-specific output
results, err := semgrep.ParseFile("semgrep-results.json")

// 2. Convert to GoVEX format
vulns := results.ToVulnerabilities()

// 3. Use standard GoVEX operations
table := vulns.Table(govex.TableColumnDefinitionSetSASTSCAReport())

docs.title: Merging Multiple Sources
docs.text:
Combine results from multiple tools:

import "github.com/grokify/govex/analyzers"

// Merge vulnerability sets from different sources
merged := analyzers.MergeVulnerabilities(
    semgrepVulns,
    spotbugsVulns,
    grypeVulns,
)

Or use the CLI:

govex merge \
  --input semgrep.json \
  --input spotbugs.json \
  --output combined.json

docs.title: CISA KEV Integration
docs.text: Integration with CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/feeds/cisakev"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The CISA KEV catalog lists vulnerabilities that are actively being exploited in the wild. This integration allows you to:

  • Download the KEV catalog
  • Check if your vulnerabilities appear in KEV
  • Prioritize remediation based on active exploitation

docs.title: Load KEV Catalog
docs.text:
// Load from CISA feed
kev, err := cisakev.LoadFromURL()
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

// Or load from local file
kev, err := cisakev.LoadFromFile("kev.json")

docs.title: Check for Known Exploited CVEs
docs.text:
// Check if a CVE is in KEV
isExploited := kev.Contains("CVE-2024-1234")

// Get KEV entry details
entry, found := kev.Get("CVE-2024-1234")
if found {
    fmt.Println("Due date:", entry.DueDate)
    fmt.Println("Vendor:", entry.VendorProject)
}

docs.title: Filter Vulnerabilities by KEV Status
docs.text:
// Get vulnerabilities that are in KEV
exploited := vulns.FilterByKEV(kev)

// Prioritize KEV vulnerabilities
for _, v := range exploited {
    fmt.Printf("URGENT: %s is actively exploited\n", v.ID)
}

docs.title: Convert to GoVEX Format
docs.text:
// Convert entire KEV catalog to vulnerabilities
vulns := kev.ToVulnerabilities()

// Generate report
table := vulns.Table(govex.TableColumnDefinitionSetSASTSCAReport())

docs.title: KEV Entry Fields
docs.text: Field Description cveID CVE identifier vendorProject Affected vendor/project product Affected product vulnerabilityName Description of the vulnerability dateAdded When added to KEV dueDate CISA remediation due date knownRansomwareCampaignUse Used in ransomware campaigns

docs.title: CISA Feed URL
docs.text:
The catalog is available at:

https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/feeds/known_exploited_vulnerabilities.json

docs.title: Prioritization Report
docs.text:
// Load your vulnerabilities
vulns, _ := govex.ReadVulnerabilitiesFile("scan-results.json")

// Load KEV
kev, _ := cisakev.LoadFromURL()

// Split by KEV status
inKEV := vulns.FilterByKEV(kev)
notInKEV := vulns.FilterNotInKEV(kev)

fmt.Printf("Actively exploited: %d\n", len(inKEV))
fmt.Printf("Not in KEV: %d\n", len(notInKEV))

docs.title: CI/CD Gate
docs.text:
// Fail build if any KEV vulnerabilities found
if len(inKEV) > 0 {
    for _, v := range inKEV {
        log.Printf("CRITICAL: %s is actively exploited", v.ID)
    }
    os.Exit(1)
}

docs.title: Semgrep Integration
docs.text: Integration with Semgrep, a fast static analysis tool.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/analyzers/semgrep"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Semgrep is a lightweight static analysis tool that supports multiple languages. The GoVEX integration converts Semgrep JSON output to the GoVEX vulnerability format.

docs.title: Supported Features
docs.text:
• CWE mapping
  • OWASP mapping
  • Severity classification
  • Source code location tracking

docs.title: Parse Semgrep Output
docs.text:
// Run Semgrep with JSON output
// semgrep --config auto --json -o results.json .

// Parse the results
results, err := semgrep.ParseFile("results.json")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

// Convert to GoVEX vulnerabilities
vulns := results.ToVulnerabilities()

docs.title: Generate Reports
docs.text:
// Create a report table
table := vulns.Table(govex.TableColumnDefinitionSetSASTSCAReport())

// Export
table.WriteXLSX("semgrep-report.xlsx")

docs.title: Basic Scan
docs.text: semgrep --config auto --json -o results.json .

docs.title: With Specific Rules
docs.text: semgrep --config p/owasp-top-ten --json -o results.json .

docs.title: CI Integration
docs.text:
# GitHub Actions example
- name: Run Semgrep
  run: |
    pip install semgrep
    semgrep --config auto --json -o semgrep.json .

- name: Process Results
  run: |
    govex merge --input semgrep.json --output vulns.json

docs.title: Field Mapping
docs.text: Semgrep Field GoVEX Field check_id ID extra.message Title extra.severity Severity extra.metadata.cwe CWE path Location.File start.line Location.Line

docs.title: SpotBugs Integration
docs.text: Integration with SpotBugs, a static analysis tool for Java.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/analyzers/spotbugs"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: SpotBugs is the successor to FindBugs, providing static analysis for Java bytecode. The GoVEX integration converts SpotBugs XML output to the GoVEX vulnerability format, focusing on security-related findings.

docs.title: Supported Features
docs.text:
• Security bug pattern detection
  • CWE mapping
  • Severity classification
  • Source code location tracking

docs.title: Parse SpotBugs Output
docs.text:
// Run SpotBugs with XML output
// spotbugs -xml -output results.xml target/classes

// Parse the results
results, err := spotbugs.ParseFile("results.xml")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

// Convert to GoVEX vulnerabilities
vulns := results.ToVulnerabilities()

docs.title: Filter Security Issues
docs.text:
// SpotBugs includes many bug types
// Filter to security-relevant findings
securityVulns := vulns.FilterCategory(govex.CategorySAST)

<plugin>

docs.title: Core Package (govex)
docs.text: The core govex package provides the fundamental types and functions for vulnerability management.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex"

docs.title: Vulnerability
docs.text:
Represents a single vulnerability finding.

type Vulnerability struct {
    ID          string
    Title       string
    Description string
    Severity    string
    Status      string
    Module      string
    Category    string
    CWE         string
    CVSS        float64
    CVSSVector  string
    DetectedOn  time.Time
    // ... additional fields
}

docs.title: Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
A slice of Vulnerability with convenience methods.

type Vulnerabilities []Vulnerability

docs.title: VulnerabilitiesSet
docs.text:
A named collection of vulnerabilities with metadata.

type VulnerabilitiesSet struct {
    Name            string
    Vulnerabilities Vulnerabilities
    Meta            VulnerabilitiesSetMeta
}

docs.title: Creating Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
vulns := govex.Vulnerabilities{
    {
        ID:       "CVE-2024-1234",
        Title:    "SQL Injection",
        Severity: "High",
        Status:   "Open",
    },
}

docs.title: Filtering
docs.text:
// Filter by severity
high := vulns.FilterSeverity("High")

// Filter by module
api := vulns.FilterModule("backend-api")

// Filter by status
open := vulns.FilterStatus("Open")

// Filter by category
sast := vulns.FilterCategory(govex.CategorySAST)

docs.title: Table Generation
docs.text:
Generate tables for reporting:

// Use predefined column set
table := vulns.Table(govex.TableColumnDefinitionSetSASTSCAReport())

// Export to formats
table.WriteXLSX("report.xlsx")
table.WriteCSV("report.csv")
md := table.Markdown()

docs.title: Statistics
docs.text:
stats := vulns.SeverityStats()
// Returns counts by severity level

docs.title: Categories
docs.text:
Predefined vulnerability categories:

Constant Description CategorySAST Static Application Security Testing CategorySCA Software Composition Analysis CategoryDAST Dynamic Application Security Testing CategoryIaC Infrastructure as Code CategoryContainer Container security

docs.title: Table Column Definitions
docs.text:
Predefined column sets for common report types:

// SAST/SCA report columns
cols := govex.TableColumnDefinitionSetSASTSCAReport()

// Custom columns
cols := []govex.TableColumnDefinition{
    {Field: govex.FieldID, Header: "CVE"},
    {Field: govex.FieldSeverity, Header: "Severity"},
    {Field: govex.FieldTitle, Header: "Title"},
}

docs.title: Reading/Writing JSON
docs.text:
// Read vulnerabilities from JSON file
vulns, err := govex.ReadVulnerabilitiesFile("vulns.json")

// Write vulnerabilities to JSON file
err := vulns.WriteFile("output.json")

docs.title: Letter Package
docs.text: The letter package generates security notification letters for vulnerability findings.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/letter"

docs.title: Letter Types
docs.text: Type Constant Description SLA Exception letter.TypeSLA Notification when remediation exceeds SLA Hardening Notice letter.TypeHardening Defense-in-depth improvement notice

docs.title: Letter
docs.text:
The main letter structure:

type Letter struct {
    SchemaVersion string
    Type          string  // "sla" or "hardening"
    Subject       string
    Sender        Sender
    CustomerName  string
    Application   string
    Finding       Finding
    CVSS          *CVSS

    // SLA-specific
    SLA          *SLA
    DelayReasons []string

    // Hardening-specific
    Rationale  string
    TargetDate string

    // Shared
    RiskAssessment   string
    Mitigations      []string
    RemediationPlan  string
    Milestones       []Milestone
    Approver         *Approver
    EscalationPolicy []string
}

docs.title: Sender
docs.text:
type Sender struct {
    Name    string
    Title   string
    Team    string
    Company string
    Email   string
    Phone   string // optional
}

docs.title: Finding
docs.text:
type Finding struct {
    Title      string
    Severity   string // Low, Moderate, High, Critical
    Identifier string
    DetectedOn string // YYYY-MM-DD
}

docs.title: SLA
docs.text:
type SLA struct {
    TargetDays      int
    OriginalDueDate string // YYYY-MM-DD
    NewDueDate      string // YYYY-MM-DD

docs.title: SLA Statuses
docs.text:
When reviewing an SLA (Service Level Agreement) to determine whether an "in-progress" item is within or out of SLA, the categorization typically uses status words or time indicators related to the lifecycle of a ticket or task. Common words or phrases include:

  1. Time-Based Categories:
  2. Within SLA: Indicates the item is still within the agreed response or resolution timeframe.
  3. Approaching SLA: Signals that the item is nearing its SLA threshold and may require immediate attention.
  4. Out of SLA: Indicates that the SLA commitment has been breached.
  5. Status Descriptors:
  6. In Progress: Work has started, and the ticket or task is being actively worked on.
  7. Pending: Waiting on an external factor, such as customer input or a third-party response. (Some SLAs pause during this status.)
  8. On Hold: Similar to "Pending," but often internally paused due to prioritization or resource constraints.
  9. Escalated: Raised to a higher tier of support or urgency due to potential SLA risks.
  10. Breached: Explicit acknowledgment that the SLA timeline was not met.
  11. Priority-Based Tags:
  12. Critical or High Priority: Often associated with shorter SLA timelines and closer monitoring.
  13. Low Priority: May have longer SLA periods, less urgency.
  14. Time-Specific Metrics:
  15. Response Time: How quickly the first response was made after the item was logged.
  16. Resolution Time: Total time taken to resolve the issue or complete the task.
  17. Elapsed Time: Time passed since the item was created, which is compared to SLA thresholds.
  18. SLA States or Indicators in Tools:
  19. Tools like ServiceNow, Jira, or Zendesk might use terms or visual indicators like:
    • Green: Within SLA.
    • Yellow/Amber: Approaching SLA breach.
    • Red: Out of SLA.

By monitoring these terms and metrics, teams can quickly identify items needing attention to ensure SLA compliance. Would you like help designing an SLA monitoring system?

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Security vulnerability statuses help track the lifecycle of a vulnerability from discovery to resolution. Here are common statuses used in vulnerability management systems:

  1. New/Identified
  2. The vulnerability has been discovered but not yet analyzed or validated.
  3. Examples:
    • A new CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) is reported.
    • An internal system scan detects a potential issue.
  4. Analyzing
  5. The vulnerability is being investigated to determine its validity, impact, and scope.
  6. Security teams assess the exploitability, risk, and affected systems.
  7. Confirmed/Validated
  8. The vulnerability is verified as legitimate and actionable.
  9. Documentation includes details such as CVE identifiers, affected versions, and potential exploit paths.
  10. Mitigated
  11. Temporary measures or workarounds have been applied to reduce the immediate risk (e.g., disabling a vulnerable service or applying a firewall rule).
  12. The issue still requires a permanent fix.
  13. In Progress
  14. Efforts to remediate the vulnerability (e.g., patch development, system updates) are underway.
  15. May include testing fixes in development or staging environments.
  16. Resolved/Fixed
  17. A patch or update has been applied to address the vulnerability permanently.
  18. Verified through testing and audit to confirm effectiveness.
  19. Remediated
  20. All affected systems and environments have been updated or patched, and the risk is eliminated.
  21. Includes a formal review to confirm that remediation was comprehensive.
  22. Closed
  23. The vulnerability is considered resolved, and no further action is required.
  24. Documentation of actions taken is archived for future reference.
  25. Reopened
  26. The issue re-emerges due to failed remediation, additional exploits, or recurrence.
  27. Indicates the need for further investigation or updated fixes.
  28. Not Applicable/False Positive
  29. The vulnerability was misclassified, irrelevant, or not exploitable in the current environment.
  30. Documented as a non-issue with justification.
  31. Deferred/Postponed
  32. Remediation is delayed due to lower priority, resource constraints, or minimal impact.
  33. Often scheduled for future resolution.
  34. Ignored/Accepted Risk
  35. The organization decides not to address the vulnerability based on an acceptable level of risk.
  36. Requires formal approval and documentation of the decision.

docs.title: Workflow Summary
docs.text:
Statuses typically follow a workflow such as:

New → Analyzing → Confirmed → In Progress → Resolved → Closed

or

New → Analyzing → Deferred/Ignored (for non-actionable cases).

docs.title: Vulnerability Reporting Formats
docs.text: This document compares common formats for vulnerability reporting, remediation tracking, and security advisories.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Several standards exist for machine-readable vulnerability information. The choice depends on the use case:

Format Best For Standard Body CycloneDX + VEX Internal triage, CI/CD pipelines, container scans OWASP CSAF Vendor security advisories, CVE publication OASIS/FIRST OSCAL FedRAMP compliance, POA&M tracking NIST SPDX + VEX License compliance with vulnerability context Linux Foundation OpenVEX Lightweight exploitability statements OpenVEX community

docs.title: CycloneDX + VEX
docs.text:
Vulnerability Exploitability eXchange (VEX) embedded in CycloneDX SBOMs.

Strengths:

  • Lightweight and CI/CD friendly
  • Native SBOM integration
  • Supports false positive justifications
  • Strong tool ecosystem (Grype, Anchore, Dependency-Track)

Use Cases:

  • Container scan triage (MS Defender, AWS Inspector)
  • Internal vulnerability management
  • DevSecOps pipelines
  • Exploitability context for specific deployments

Tool Support: Grype, Anchore, IntelliJ, VS Code, Dependency-Track

docs.title: CSAF (Common Security Advisory Framework)
docs.text:
OASIS standard for structured security advisories, successor to CVRF.

Strengths:

  • Purpose-built for CVE advisories
  • Rich metadata (CPE, PURL, CVSS, CWE)
  • Supports signed advisories and public feeds
  • Designed for downstream automation

Use Cases:

  • Vendor security bulletins
  • Public CVE disclosure
  • Security advisory aggregation
  • Threat intelligence feeds

Adopters: Red Hat, Cisco, Siemens, SUSE, GitHub

Limitations:

  • Heavyweight for internal triage
  • Not optimized for "is this exploitable in my environment?" workflows
  • Less tight SBOM integration

docs.title: OSCAL (Open Security Controls Assessment Language)
docs.text:
NIST standard for security assessment and compliance documentation.

Strengths:

  • FedRAMP/NIST RMF alignment
  • POA&M (Plan of Action & Milestones) support
  • Structured compliance documentation
  • System Security Plan (SSP) automation

Use Cases:

  • FedRAMP authorization packages
  • NIST 800-53 control assessment
  • Compliance tracking and auditing
  • GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance) workflows

Limitations:

  • Not designed for CVE-centric reporting
  • Lacks CVSS, exploitability indicators
  • No affected product enumeration (CPE/PURL)
  • Limited use outside regulated compliance

Note: OSCAL POA&M can track vulnerabilities as "weaknesses" but lacks the structured CVE data that CSAF provides.

docs.title: SPDX + VEX
docs.text:
Linux Foundation SBOM standard with VEX extension.

Strengths:

  • Strong foundation for license compliance
  • Linux Foundation backing
  • Growing VEX support

Limitations:

  • VEX extension less mature than CycloneDX
  • Not yet dominant in container vulnerability workflows

docs.title: OpenVEX
docs.text:
Google-backed lightweight VEX format.

Strengths:

  • Simpler than full SBOM+VEX
  • Focused purely on exploitability statements

Limitations:

  • Early adoption phase
  • Limited tool support currently

docs.title: Recommendations by Use Case
docs.text: Use Case Recommended Format Container scan triage CycloneDX + VEX CI/CD vulnerability gates CycloneDX + VEX Public CVE advisories CSAF Vendor security bulletins CSAF FedRAMP compliance OSCAL POA&M tracking OSCAL License + vulnerability SPDX + VEX

docs.title: Multi-Format Publication
docs.text:
Many vendors publish advisories in multiple formats:

Format Availability Purpose HTML/PDF Common Human-readable JSON API Common Automation CSAF Growing Machine-readable advisories CycloneDX + VEX Rare DevSecOps consumption

docs.title: Vendor Examples
docs.text:
• Red Hat: CSAF feed, web advisories, JSON/YAML APIs
  • Cisco: CSAF feed, HTML advisories, RSS feeds
  • Siemens: CSAF feed, PDF reports

docs.title: CSAF vs OSCAL for Vulnerability Reporting
docs.text:
Aspect CSAF OSCAL POA&M Primary purpose CVE advisories Compliance tracking Audience Security teams, vendors Auditors, GRC teams CVE structure Native support Generic "weaknesses" CVSS support Native Not included Scanner integration Strong Limited Remediation detail Patches, workarounds High-level plans

Summary: CSAF is designed for vulnerability disclosure; OSCAL is designed for compliance documentation. While OSCAL POA&M can reference vulnerabilities, it lacks the structured CVE data and tooling integration that CSAF provides.

docs.text:

  • CSAF (OASIS)
  • OSCAL (NIST)
  • SPDX
  • OpenVEX
  • VEX Status Justifications

docs.title: Pentest Remediation Status Reports
docs.text: The new reports/pentest package provides types and utilities for generating pentest remediation status letters:

docs.title: Report Structure
docs.text:
• Metadata - Assessment details including assessor, dates, scope
  • Findings Summary - Counts by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low)

  • Pending Items - Findings still awaiting remediation with owner and target dates

import "github.com/grokify/govex/reports/pentest"

// Create a new report
report := pentest.NewReport("Q2 2026 Pentest Remediation Status")
report.Metadata = pentest.Metadata{
    ReportName:     "External Network Penetration Test - Q2 2026",

    AssessmentDate: "2026-04-15",

    Author:         "Security Team",

}

// Set findings counts
report.Findings = pentest.FindingsSummary{
    Critical: 2,

    Low:      12,
}

// Add pending items

        Severity:   "Critical",
        Status:     "In Progress",

    },
}

// Calculate status and generate output

docs.title: Pentest Remediation Status Reports
docs.text: The reports/pentest package provides types and utilities for generating pentest remediation status letters that track penetration test findings, compensating controls, and remediation milestones.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Pentest remediation status reports help organizations communicate the progress of fixing security findings from penetration tests. These reports include:

  • Findings Summary - Counts by severity level
  • Remediation Status - Fixed vs. pending with completion percentage
  • Pending Items - Details on findings still being remediated
  • Compensating Controls - Mitigations in place for pending items
  • Milestones - Upcoming remediation phases

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
import "github.com/grokify/govex/reports/pentest"

// Create a new report
report := pentest.NewReport("Q2 2026 External Pentest Remediation Status")

// Set metadata
report.Metadata = pentest.Metadata{
    ReportName:     "External Network Penetration Test - Q2 2026",
    Assessor:       "SecureCo Consulting",
    AssessmentDate: "2026-04-15",
    ReportDate:     "2026-06-15",
    Author:         "Security Team",
    Scope:          "External-facing web applications and APIs",
}

// Set findings counts by severity
report.Findings = pentest.FindingsSummary{
    Critical: 2,
    High:     5,
    Medium:   8,
    Low:      12,
}

// Add pending items (findings still being remediated)
report.PendingItems = []pentest.PendingItem{
    {
        ID:               "PENTEST-001",
        Title:            "SQL Injection in Search API",
        Severity:         "Critical",
        Status:           "In Progress",
        Owner:            "Platform Team",
        TargetDate:       "2026-07-01",
        CompensatingCtrl: "CC-001",
    },
    {
        ID:             "PENTEST-003",
        Title:          "Missing Rate Limiting on Auth Endpoint",
        Severity:       "High",
        Status:         "Blocked",
        Owner:          "API Team",
        TargetDate:     "2026-07-15",
        BlockingReason: "Waiting for API gateway upgrade",
    },
}

// Add compensating controls
report.CompensatingControls = []pentest.CompensatingControl{
    {
        ID:             "CC-001",
        Title:          "WAF SQL Injection Rules",
        Description:    "ModSecurity rules deployed to block common SQL injection patterns.",
        FindingIDs:     []string{"PENTEST-001"},
        ImplementedOn:  "2026-04-20",
        EffectivenessS: "High - blocks 99% of known attack patterns",
    },
}

// Add milestones
report.NextMilestones = []pentest.Milestone{
    {
        Phase:       1,
        Title:       "Critical Findings Remediation",
        Description: "Fix all critical severity findings",
        TargetDate:  "2026-07-01",
        FindingIDs:  []string{"PENTEST-001", "PENTEST-002"},
        Owner:       "Platform Team",
    },
    {
        Phase:       2,
        Title:       "High Findings Remediation",
        Description: "Fix all high severity findings",
        TargetDate:  "2026-07-31",
        Owner:       "Security Team",
    },
}

// Calculate status from findings and pending items
report.CalculateStatus()

// Write to file

docs.text:

    Metadata             Metadata
    Findings             FindingsSummary
    Status               RemediationSummary
    PendingItems         []PendingItem
    CompensatingControls []CompensatingControl
    NextMilestones       []Milestone
}

docs.title: Metadata
docs.text:
type Metadata struct {
    ReportName     string  // Name of the pentest report
    Assessor       string  // Assessment firm or assessor
    AssessmentDate string  // Date of assessment (YYYY-MM-DD)
    ReportDate     string  // Date of this status report
    Author         string  // Author of this report
    Scope          string  // Scope of the assessment
}

docs.title: FindingsSummary
docs.text:
type FindingsSummary struct {
    Critical int
    High     int
    Medium   int
    Low      int
}

// Methods
func (f FindingsSummary) Total() int

docs.title: RemediationSummary
docs.text:
type RemediationSummary struct {
    Total   int
    Fixed   int
    Pending int
}

// Methods
func (r RemediationSummary) PercentComplete() float64
func (r RemediationSummary) PercentCompleteString() string

docs.title: PendingItem
docs.text:
type PendingItem struct {
    ID               string  // Finding identifier
    Title            string  // Finding title
    Severity         string  // Critical, High, Medium, Low
    Status           string  // Current status
    Owner            string  // Team or person responsible
    TargetDate       string  // Target remediation date
    Notes            string  // Additional notes
    BlockingReason   string  // Reason if blocked
    CompensatingCtrl string  // Reference to compensating control ID
}

docs.title: CompensatingControl
docs.text:
type CompensatingControl struct {
    ID             string   // Control identifier
    Title          string   // Control title
    Description    string   // Description of the control
    FindingIDs     []string // IDs of findings this control addresses
    ImplementedOn  string   // Date control was implemented
    EffectivenessS string   // Assessment of control effectiveness
}

docs.title: Milestone
docs.text:
type Milestone struct {
    Phase          int      // Phase number
    Title          string   // Milestone title
    Description    string   // Milestone description
    TargetDate     string   // Target completion date
    FindingIDs     []string // IDs of findings addressed
    Owner          string   // Team or person responsible
    CustomerImpact string   // Expected impact to customers
}

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text: See the example command for generated output samples in JSON, Markdown, and PDF formats.

docs.title: POAM (Plan of Action & Milestones)
docs.text: Generate FedRAMP-compliant Plan of Action and Milestones documents.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/reports/poam"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
POA&M (Plan of Action and Milestones) is a document required for FedRAMP authorization that tracks:

  • Known system weaknesses
  • Planned remediation actions
  • Target completion dates
  • Responsible parties

docs.title: FedRAMP Context
docs.text: POA&M is part of the FedRAMP (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) compliance framework. It documents how an organization plans to address security findings.

docs.title: Create POAM Entry
docs.text:
entry := poam.Entry{
    ID:              "POAM-001",
    Weakness:        "SQL Injection vulnerability in login form",
    PointOfContact:  "Security Team",
    Resources:       "2 developers, 1 sprint",
    ScheduledDate:   "2026-06-30",
    MilestoneChanges: "None",
    Status:          "In Progress",
}

docs.title: From Vulnerabilities
docs.text: entries := vulns.ToPOAMEntries()

docs.title: POAM Fields
docs.text: Field Description ID Unique identifier Weakness Description of the finding Point of Contact Responsible party Resources Required resources Scheduled Completion Target date Milestone Changes Updates to timeline Status Current status Comments Additional notes

docs.title: OSCAL Integration
docs.text:
POA&M is part of the OSCAL (Open Security Controls Assessment Language) standard. GoVEX POA&M output can be integrated with OSCAL workflows.

See Vulnerability Formats for OSCAL vs CSAF comparison.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Vulnerability Formats
  • SLA Management

docs.title: PSIRT Report
docs.text: Generate Product Security Incident Response Team (PSIRT) reports.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/reports/psirtreport"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
PSIRT reports provide structured vulnerability summaries for security teams. Reports include:

  • Vulnerability counts by severity
  • Statistics by reporter (internal/external)
  • Module-level breakdown
  • Trend analysis

docs.title: Generate Report
docs.text: report := psirtreport.Report(vulnSet)

docs.title: Report Sections
docs.text:
The report includes:

  1. Executive Summary - High-level counts and trends
  2. By Severity - Breakdown by severity level
  3. By Reporter - Internal vs external findings
  4. By Module - Per-component statistics
  5. Details - Full vulnerability listing

docs.title: Reporter Filtering
docs.text:
Filter by reporter type:

// External reporters (bug bounty, pen tests, etc.)
external := vulnSet.FilterReporterExternal()

// Internal reporters (SAST, SCA, internal review)
internal := vulnSet.FilterReporterInternal()

docs.title: Statistics
docs.text:
stats := vulns.SeverityStats()
// Returns: map[severity]count

statsByReporter := vulns.SeverityStatsSetByReporter()
// Returns: map[reporter]SeverityStats

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Core Package - Vulnerability filtering
  • Severity Package - Statistics types

docs.title: Release BOM
docs.text: Generate Release Bill of Materials with vulnerability tracking.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/reports/releasebom"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Release BOM creates manifests that track vulnerabilities across container images and modules in a release. This is useful for:

  • Release approval workflows
  • Security sign-off documentation
  • Compliance tracking
  • Container vulnerability summaries

docs.title: Create Release BOM
docs.text:
bom := releasebom.ReleaseBOM{
    Name:    "my-service",
    Version: "1.2.3",
    Date:    time.Now(),
    Images:  []releasebom.Image{...},
}

docs.title: From Vulnerability Set
docs.text:
// Map modules to container image names
moduleToImage := map[string]string{
    "backend-api": "myregistry/backend:1.2.3",
    "frontend":    "myregistry/frontend:1.2.3",
}

bom := vulns.ReleaseBOMModuleToImageName(moduleToImage)

docs.title: Severity Counts by Module
docs.text:
counts := vulns.SeverityCountSetsByModule()
// Returns: map[module]SeverityCounts

docs.title: Markdown
docs.text: md := bom.Markdown()

docs.title: YAML
docs.text: yaml := bom.YAML()

docs.title: BOM Structure
docs.text:
name: my-service
version: 1.2.3
date: 2026-04-11
images:
  - name: myregistry/backend:1.2.3
    module: backend-api
    vulnerabilities:
      critical: 0
      high: 2
      medium: 5
      low: 10
  - name: myregistry/frontend:1.2.3
    module: frontend
    vulnerabilities:
      critical: 0
      high: 0
      medium: 3
      low: 8

docs.title: Site Writer
docs.text: Generate Markdown websites for vulnerability tracking.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/reports/sitewriter"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Site Writer creates Markdown documentation sites for tracking vulnerabilities across multiple projects. Output is compatible with:

  • Git repository viewers (GitHub, GitLab)
  • MkDocs
  • Other static site generators

docs.title: Generate Site for Repository
docs.text:
writer := sitewriter.New()

// Write vulnerability pages for a repo
err := vulnSet.WriteFilesSiteForRepo(writer, "my-project")

docs.title: CLI Usage
docs.text:
# Generate homepage
govex homepage --output docs/index.md

docs.title: Site Structure
docs.text:
Generated site structure:

docs/
├── index.md              # Homepage with summary
├── projects/
│   ├── project-a/
│   │   ├── index.md      # Project summary
│   │   └── 2024-01/      # Monthly reports
│   │       └── index.md
│   └── project-b/
│       └── ...
└── reports/
    └── ...

docs.title: Homepage Generation
docs.text:
The homepage aggregates statistics across all projects:

// Generate homepage content
homepage := writer.Homepage(projects)

// Write to file
err := os.WriteFile("docs/index.md", []byte(homepage), 0644)

docs.title: Merge Command
docs.text:
Combine multiple JSON files for site generation:

# Merge vulnerability files
govex merge \
  --input project-a.json \
  --input project-b.json \
  --output combined.json

# Then generate site

docs.title: Integration with MkDocs
docs.text:
The generated Markdown works with MkDocs:

# mkdocs.yml
nav:
  - Home: index.md
  - Projects:
    - Project A: projects/project-a/index.md
    - Project B: projects/project-b/index.md

docs.title: Standards Overview
docs.text: GoVEX supports common vulnerability and security standards.

docs.title: Supported Standards
docs.text: Standard Package Description CVE 2.0 standards/cve20 NIST CVE format CVSS standards/cvss30 Vulnerability scoring CWE standards/cwe Weakness enumeration CSAF standards/csaf Security advisories

docs.title: Standards Comparison
docs.text: For a detailed comparison of vulnerability reporting formats, see Vulnerability Formats.

docs.title: Usage Pattern
docs.text:
All standards packages follow a similar pattern:

// Parse standard format
data, err := cve20.ParseFile("cve.json")

// Convert to GoVEX format
vulns := data.ToVulnerabilities()

// Use standard GoVEX operations
table := vulns.Table(govex.TableColumnDefinitionSetSASTSCAReport())

docs.title: Integration with NVD
docs.text:
The nvd package provides an API client for the NIST National Vulnerability Database:

import "github.com/grokify/govex/nvd"

client := nvd.NewClient(apiKey)
cve, err := client.GetCVE("CVE-2024-1234")

docs.title: CSAF Support
docs.text: Support for Common Security Advisory Framework (CSAF).

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/standards/csaf"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: CSAF is an OASIS standard for machine-readable security advisories. It's the successor to CVRF (Common Vulnerability Reporting Framework) and is widely used by vendors for publishing security bulletins.

docs.title: When to Use CSAF
docs.text:
CSAF is best for:

  • Publishing security advisories to customers
  • Vendor CVE disclosure
  • Security bulletin automation
  • Threat intelligence feeds

For internal vulnerability triage, consider CycloneDX + VEX instead. See Vulnerability Formats for comparison.

docs.title: Parse CSAF Document
docs.text:
advisory, err := csaf.ParseFile("advisory.json")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

fmt.Println("Title:", advisory.Document.Title)
fmt.Println("Publisher:", advisory.Document.Publisher.Name)

docs.title: Convert to GoVEX
docs.text: vulns := advisory.ToVulnerabilities()

docs.title: CSAF Document Structure
docs.text:
{
  "document": {
    "title": "Security Advisory",
    "publisher": {
      "name": "Vendor Name",
      "category": "vendor"
    },
    "tracking": {
      "id": "VENDOR-2024-001",
      "status": "final",
      "version": "1.0"
    }
  },
  "vulnerabilities": [
    {
      "cve": "CVE-2024-1234",
      "title": "Buffer Overflow",
      "scores": [
        {
          "cvss_v3": {
            "baseScore": 9.8,
            "vectorString": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H"
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "product_tree": {
    "branches": [...]
  }
}

docs.title: CSAF Feeds
docs.text:
Major vendors publishing CSAF feeds:

Vendor Feed URL Red Hat https://access.redhat.com/security/data/csaf/v2/advisories/ Cisco https://sec.cloudapps.cisco.com/security-pack/csa Siemens https://www.siemens.com/cert/advisories/csaf

docs.title: Profiles
docs.text:
CSAF defines several profiles:

Profile Use Case Informational General security information Security Incident Response Incident details Security Advisory Vulnerability advisories VEX Exploitability statements

docs.title: CVE 2.0 Support
docs.text: Support for the NIST CVE 2.0 JSON format.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/standards/cve20"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) is the standard identifier for security vulnerabilities. GoVEX supports parsing CVE 2.0 JSON format used by NVD and other sources.

docs.title: Parse CVE JSON
docs.text:
cve, err := cve20.ParseFile("CVE-2024-1234.json")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

fmt.Println("CVE ID:", cve.ID)
fmt.Println("Description:", cve.Description)

docs.title: Convert to GoVEX
docs.text: vuln := cve.ToVulnerability()

docs.title: CVE Fields
docs.text: Field Description cveId CVE identifier (e.g., CVE-2024-1234) descriptions Multi-language descriptions metrics CVSS scores weaknesses CWE mappings references External references affected Affected products

docs.title: NVD API Client
docs.text:
Query the NVD directly:

import "github.com/grokify/govex/nvd"

client := nvd.NewClient(apiKey)

// Get single CVE
cve, err := client.GetCVE("CVE-2024-1234")

// Search CVEs
results, err := client.SearchCVEs(nvd.SearchParams{
    KeywordSearch: "apache",
    PubStartDate:  "2024-01-01",
})

docs.text:

docs.title: CVSS Support
docs.text: Support for Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS).

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/standards/cvss30"

docs.title: Supported Versions
docs.text: Version Support CVSS 3.0 Full CVSS 3.1 Full CVSS 4.0 Partial

docs.title: Parse CVSS Vector
docs.text:
score, err := cvss30.ParseVector("CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}

fmt.Println("Base Score:", score.BaseScore())
fmt.Println("Severity:", score.Severity())

docs.title: Calculate Score
docs.text:
// From vector components
score := cvss30.Score{
    AttackVector:       "Network",
    AttackComplexity:   "Low",
    PrivilegesRequired: "None",
    UserInteraction:    "None",
    Scope:              "Unchanged",
    Confidentiality:    "High",
    Integrity:          "High",
    Availability:       "High",
}

base := score.Calculate()

docs.title: Severity Ratings
docs.text: Score Range Rating 0.0 None 0.1 - 3.9 Low 4.0 - 6.9 Medium 7.0 - 8.9 High 9.0 - 10.0 Critical

docs.title: Vector String Format
docs.text:
CVSS 3.1 vector format:

CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H
Metric Values AV (Attack Vector) N (Network), A (Adjacent), L (Local), P (Physical) AC (Attack Complexity) L (Low), H (High) PR (Privileges Required) N (None), L (Low), H (High) UI (User Interaction) N (None), R (Required) S (Scope) U (Unchanged), C (Changed) C (Confidentiality) N (None), L (Low), H (High) I (Integrity) N (None), L (Low), H (High) A (Availability) N (None), L (Low), H (High)

docs.text:

docs.title: CWE Support
docs.text: Support for Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE).

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: import "github.com/grokify/govex/standards/cwe"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: CWE is a community-developed list of software and hardware weakness types. GoVEX supports parsing CWE identifiers and XML data.

docs.title: Parse CWE ID
docs.text:
// Parse CWE identifier
id, err := cwe.ParseCWEAsPrefix("CWE-79")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
fmt.Println("CWE Number:", id) // 79

docs.title: Parse Multiple CWEs
docs.text:
// Parse multiple CWE identifiers
ids := cwe.ParseCWEsAsPrefix([]string{"CWE-79", "CWE-89", "CWE-22"})

docs.title: Common CWEs
docs.text: CWE Name Category CWE-79 Cross-site Scripting (XSS) Injection CWE-89 SQL Injection Injection CWE-22 Path Traversal Input Validation CWE-78 OS Command Injection Injection CWE-352 Cross-Site Request Forgery Session Management CWE-287 Improper Authentication Authentication CWE-862 Missing Authorization Authorization CWE-798 Hard-coded Credentials Credentials CWE-200 Information Exposure Information Leak CWE-502 Deserialization Input Validation
